Dispatch-generics verdict: the keep-shape must not reopen the settled skeleton — static specialization as the ONLY implicit path. Any implicit closure boxing or implicit coercion to function-pointer/dyn types would violate it; my restriction routes all erasure through the explicit D003-style construct. The judge already lists closure/function-value dispatch as required follow-up work, so this trial's outcome feeds that gap.

IR-strategy verdict (research_needed): the value of compiler-owned capture facts assumes they survive lowering and are consumed by passes — the round1 index lists exactly this (emitted facts surviving lowering) as an unvalidated common gap; if that program fails, the optimizer-necessity half of my case weakens, though the verification half stands.

Violation-semantics direction: checked capture lists are compiler-proven contracts (assumable in every build mode under the leading candidate); if that verdict shifts to treating more contracts as writer-asserted, the closure advantage over writer-asserted env structs narrows.
Concurrency-model verdict: I concede near-parity — Send/Sync-style auto-traits check plain env structs as well as closures (C004), so closures must not be defended on race-safety grounds.
empiricalUntested: EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED: that AI writers actually drift more on N near-duplicate env-struct/packing blocks than on inline closures — the corpus contains zero AI-codegen evidence; the edit-amplification factor (1 site vs 3+ sites) is structural/analytic, but the resulting error RATE is unmeasured.
EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED: that AI writers insert semantics-changing clones/copies to appease ownership checks at higher rates under manual env-packing than under compiler capture.
EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED: the actual token/context-cost multiplier of defunctionalized style over closure style in generated codebases, and its effect on iteration-loop quality.

UNQUANTIFIED (D001 negative scope): compile-time and code-size of monomorphizing closure-heavy code vs many top-level defunctionalized items — compile-time is not established as an axis favoring either side.

UNQUANTIFIED (corpus-wide gap per round1 index): no measured runtime performance delta between guaranteed direct calls via unique closure types and writer-specialized manual clones or recovered devirtualized calls; the asymmetry cited is qualitative (guaranteed vs conditional per D007/misleading-comparisons #3), not benchmarked.

EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED: that explicit compiler-verified capture lists reduce AI capture errors relative to pure inference — the proposed keep-shape's key mitigation is itself an untested hypothesis about machine-writer behavior.

UNVALIDATED: that capture-derived facts (exclusive-capture noalias etc.) survive lowering to LLVM/MLIR and are consumed by the passes that matter (F003/F009 are conditional carriers; misleading-comparisons #5).

result.stance: CUT. Type inference and implicit conversions are excluded from the core language. The core is full mandatory annotation of every type, effect, layout, and mode at every binding and call; the compiler is a local checker plus a truthful lowering table, never an intent-guesser. Critical scoping: sound below-source optimizer analysis that derives ADDITIONAL facts (known-bits, ranges, alias refinement) is not the accused feature and stays; what is cut is any mechanism where the compiler DETERMINES source semantics — what type a binding has, or which conversion gets inserted.
result.coreArgument: The charter says the writer is fallible at scale and verification matters more than in human languages. Type inference and implicit conversions are, precisely, mechanisms for trusting the writer's unstated intent: inference manufactures source-level facts by constraint-solving over text the writer never committed to, and implicit conversions actively launder the canonical hallucination artifact — a type mismatch — into compiling code with silently changed semantics (truncation, precision loss, mode change). Four admissible attacks. (1) Contract truthfulness: the corpus's best-verified finding class says optimizer facts are UB-bearing promises, sound only if frontend obligations are actually true (F001, F003; misleading-comparisons note 5: 'IR metadata is sound only if frontend semantics/proofs are sound'). Full annotation makes the source itself the fact ledger — every fact has a declaring token, is locally checkable, and yields an exact machine-readable diagnostic at the site of inconsistency. Inference has no oracle: nothing can verify the solver guessed the writer's INTENT, only that its guess is internally consistent, so the trusted computing base grows by exactly the component whose failure mode is silent. (2) Guarantee vs conditional recovery: the corpus's one repeatedly-confirmed qualitative asymmetry is that source-level guarantees beat conditional, revertible recovery (F007's escape analysis is conditional elimination, not a guarantee; the round1 dispatch-generics verdict crowns 'guaranteed static specialization as the core's only implicit path'). Source type inference is conditional recovery relocated into the frontend: Julia, the flagship inference-for-performance design, recovers performance only when types happen to be stable and concrete, and fails SILENTLY into dynamic dispatch and indirect storage when they do not (D009, D010). In an extreme-performance language a silent performance cliff is a defect; mandatory annotation makes type stability structural rather than emergent, and feeds monomorphization (D001) with declared concrete types. The J-series shows the same lesson from the other side: the optimizer's information demand is constant, only the channel differs (J001), and every channel other than explicit static declaration buys guard/invalidation machinery (J002) to reconstruct what a static language could simply guarantee (J003). (3) Verifiability and compile time in the AI iteration loop: with full annotation the checker is local, embarrassingly parallel, and incrementally verifiable in the style the corpus already trusts (N006's Alive2-backed, flag-sensitive folds). Declared interfaces are fixed separate-compilation boundaries — a body edit can never ripple inferred types across modules — whereas global inference couples every downstream unit's types to upstream bodies (D004 confirms the unit-boundary/compile-time/optimization coupling is real). (4) Numeric ground truth: round1-numeric-semantics ended no_decision, its critic found 'all mixed-mode conversion rules are invention' — zero corpus evidence exists for ANY sound implicit-conversion design — and its leading candidate is explicitly named, truthfully-lowered modes. Implicit conversion between modes is silent injection of nsw/nuw-grade optimization promises with poison consequences (N001, N002): a standing soundness hazard by that debate's own ground rules. Finally, the machine-writer ledger favors the cut where it matters most: inference maximizes the semantic blast radius of minimal textual perturbation (a hallucinated 0 vs 0.0 silently retypes an entire inference region and still compiles), while mandatory annotation makes the same perturbation collide with a declared fact and die as one local diagnostic. Annotation converts consistency drift from a silent semantic hazard into a compile error — the correct direction for a writer that cannot be trusted with unchecked invariants. Verbosity is charter-admissible; guessing is not. Under the null hypothesis, the defense must produce evidence that inference earns its verifier complexity and its silence — the corpus contains none.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: LLVM noalias/alias.scope facts are undefined behavior if violated — optimizer facts are contracts sound only when frontend obligations are true. Facts must therefore be declared and locally checked, not manufactured by an intent-guessing solver whose errors are silently accepted. (notes/workflow-confirmed-findings.jsonl)

Language-level non-interference rules are central optimizer enablers, and restrict-like contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness' — the whole confirmed enabler class presupposes truthful declared facts, which mandatory annotation supplies and inference dilutes.

HotSpot escape analysis gives conditional elimination, not guaranteed stack allocation — the corpus's confirmed asymmetry: guarantees beat conditional recovery. Source type inference is conditional recovery in the frontend; annotation is the guarantee.

Julia recovers performance via inference+specialization only when types are stable and concrete; abstract or changing types silently hide representation/call targets and cause dynamic dispatch. Documented proof that failed inference degrades performance silently rather than erroring — a defect under extreme-performance goals.

World age and unbounded instantiation cases constrain Julia's inference-driven specialization model — the inference path carries standing optimizer caveats that a declared-type core simply does not have.

Monomorphization compiles concrete type-specific bodies with concrete-code performance — the payoff mechanism consumes declared concrete types directly; annotation feeds it without an inference stage. (Negative scope: compile-time/code-size unquantified — conceded.)

Compilation-unit boundaries trade compile time against optimization — evidence the boundary structure matters. Mandatory annotated interfaces give fixed separate-compilation boundaries for the AI iteration loop; global inference makes downstream types depend on upstream bodies.

Dynamic runtimes must recover types via profiling+guards; per its positive scope 'the information demand is the same, only the channel differs.' Explicit static declaration is the only channel with no guard, profile, or solver machinery.
Speculative type recovery requires invalidation machinery — per its positive scope, exactly 'the concrete mechanisms and costs a source-level-contract language could avoid by making facts static.'

Stable object layout is the optimizer-relevant property; dynamic systems reconstruct it, 'static languages could guarantee it.' Mandatory layout annotation at every binding is that guarantee in its strongest form.

LLVM's definedness ordering (UB/poison/undef/frozen/concrete) is an explicit optimization contract; conversions move values between definedness classes, so implicitly inserted conversions decide contract semantics the writer never stated.

nsw/nuw are optimization promises whose violation yields poison — implicit numeric conversion/promotion is silent injection of optimization promises; the debate's own ground rules call ungated promise injection a standing soundness hazard.

InstCombine's flag-sensitive folds are expected to be Alive2-verified — the corpus's model of trustworthy compilation is small, mechanically checkable transformation rules. A checker of declared annotations fits this model; an intent-reconstruction solver has no Alive2 analogue.

'IR metadata solves source design' is flagged misleading because metadata is sound only if frontend semantics/proofs are sound and preserved — the frontend must not contain an unverifiable guessing component upstream of fact emission.

Critic finding: 'all mixed-mode conversion rules are invention' — implicit conversion design has zero card support anywhere in the corpus; the leading candidate is named, truthfully-lowered explicit modes. Under the standing rule in notes/caveats.md, an unevidenced core feature stays out.

Verdict candidate: guaranteed static specialization as the core's ONLY implicit path, prized specifically for source-level guarantees over conditional recovery — the same principle that convicts frontend type inference.

machineWriterCosts: Generation token inflation: every binding and call carries type/effect/layout/mode text; the multiplier over inferred code is unmeasured. Admissible and conceded — but verbosity is explicitly charter-payable, and annotations are mechanically generable.

Context-window pressure: annotated code is longer per function, so fewer functions fit per window; possible second-order hallucination increase (EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED). Offsetting credit: every line is self-describing, so re-reading and repairing a call site requires zero surrounding derivation context, and comprehension happens on every re-read while generation happens once (EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED both directions).

Iteration-loop error volume: mandatory annotation converts silent drift into loud compile errors, raising the count of compile-fix cycles; each error is local, anchored to a declaring token, and machine-readable — the cheapest error class for automated repair (repair-rate claim EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED).
Refactor fanout: an interface change forces annotated edits at every call site — more tokens per refactor, though every edit is locally checkable and mechanically derivable from the new signature.

Costs the cut AVOIDS: no non-local inference diagnostics reported far from their cause to misdirect automated repair; no single-token hallucination (0 vs 0.0) silently retyping an entire inference region while still compiling; no implicit conversion laundering a mismatch hallucination into compiling-but-wrong code with changed numeric mode or precision.

Compile-time in the loop: local checking against declared interfaces is parallel and incremental with no cross-module type ripples; global inference solvers add a fixed-point/constraint stage with known pathological worst cases (worst-case claim is outside-corpus general PL knowledge; corpus has no compile-time quantification per D001/D004 negative scopes).
consistencyRisks: Annotation drift across near-duplicate code copies: repeated annotation blocks can drift during generation, BUT drift collides with declared facts elsewhere and dies as a local compile error — the alternative converts the charter's named drift hazard from silent (inference quietly assigns each near-duplicate its own types and both compile) to checked. Residual risk: drift that remains internally consistent within one copy.

Consistent-but-wrong annotation sets: the checker verifies coherence, not intent; a hallucinated-but-coherent set (wrong mode declared everywhere) type-checks. Note this risk is strictly worse under inference, which removes the redundant declarations that give cross-checking any purchase at all.

Performance-pessimizing over-annotation: defensively wide modes/effects (wrapping arithmetic everywhere, maximal effect rows) are sound, consistent, and slow; no core checker catches pessimization — requires cost-diagnostic tooling outside the core, and mirrors D009's over-specialization caveat in inverted form.
Systematic template copying: an AI writer reusing annotation boilerplate across contexts could produce correlated errors that all type-check (EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED).
Redundant fact statement at binding and call multiplies sites that must be co-edited on change; stale sites are cheap local compile errors but inflate iteration count.
empiricalUntested: All claims about AI writer behavior in this argument — annotation-generation accuracy, drift rates across near-duplicates, whether local diagnostics improve automated repair success over non-local inference diagnostics, and relative context cost of reading annotated vs inferred code — the corpus contains zero AI-codegen evidence.
The token-cost multiplier of full mandatory annotation on generation and on re-reading, and whether it pushes enough code out of context windows to raise hallucination rates elsewhere.
Whether redundant annotations catch hallucinations at a rate exceeding the additional hallucinations introduced by generating more annotation tokens (the cross-check-vs-doubling question).

Compile-time comparison: global inference solving vs parsing/checking a much larger annotated source; the corpus explicitly lacks compile-time quantification anywhere (D001/D004 negative scopes), and inference-solver worst cases are outside-corpus general knowledge.

The 'fourth fact channel' from round1-numeric-semantics: how much nsw/range-grade optimization the compiler can soundly infer from declared types and dominating guards with no extra frontend flags — uncarded in both directions; if large, it reprices how much mode annotation the optimizer actually needs (it does not license source-semantics inference, but it bounds the annotation burden).
Silent performance-cliff magnitude from failed inference: documented qualitatively for Julia (D009) but unquantified there and unmeasured for any AOT inference design.

That implicit conversions historically caused optimizer-visible defect classes (C promotions, Fortran mixed-mode): plausible, widely believed, and NOT carded in this corpus — the prosecution's implicit-conversion case rests on the absence of supporting evidence plus N001/N002 hazard structure, not on carded failure history.

result.stance: SPLIT VERDICT — PARTIAL KEEP. I defend bounded type inference: mandatory full annotation at every function/module signature, public data layout, FFI boundary, and UB-bearing contract surface; deterministic, policy-free inference only inside function bodies, anchored to those signatures. I CONCEDE implicit value-changing conversions entirely — they are indefensible under this charter and should be cut; the only coercions kept are representation-identical, statically checked mode weakenings (e.g., exclusive-to-shared reference). Whole-program/global inference is also conceded. I do not defend inference on ergonomics; I defend it because the alternative is either unsound or incoherent, and its coherent form maximizes the machine-writer failure surface.
result.coreArgument: The alternative — trusted-or-checked fork — collapses into keeping inference. Mandatory annotation of every type, effect, layout, and mode at every binding and call faces a dilemma. Branch (a): annotations are TRUSTED by the optimizer. Then a fallible AI writer at scale feeds UB-bearing contract channels directly: F003 noalias/alias.scope is UB on violation, N002 nsw/nsw makes overflow poison, N004 range metadata poisons out-of-range values. The corpus's own discipline forbids this: misleading-comparisons note 5 says IR metadata is sound only if frontend proofs are sound, and the round1-violation-semantics post-critique convergence is that proven facts are assumable everywhere while UNPROVEN ASSERTION IS NEVER AMBIENT. An AI annotation is an unproven assertion; with a writer fallible at scale, trusted-annotation-everywhere is the 'unsound frontend metadata emission' historical failure institutionalized as the language design. This is the verification-necessity leg: soundness requires the compiler to prove these facts independently. Branch (b): annotations are CHECKED. But checking an annotation requires the compiler to independently derive the type/effect/mode of the annotated term — the derivation engine (inference) must exist in the core REGARDLESS. The 'cut' therefore does not remove inference; it only forbids surface elision, converting every binding and call into a redundant checked assertion. The debate reduces to: does total surface redundancy pay? All remaining considerations are machine-writer costs, where the alternative loses (see machineWriterCosts). Optimizer-necessity leg: three concrete losses under the alternative. (1) Unnameable types: monomorphized generic intermediates (D001) and closures are compiler-constructed identities with no stable surface name. Mandating a writable type at every binding forces either nominalization/erasure to function-pointer or dyn representations — carded costs: D002 (vtable dispatch prevents inlining), D009 (Function-typed fields hide call targets and force indirect storage even in closed programs) — or the language adds opaque/existential annotations, which readmit inference under another name. A strict reading of the alternative is either performance-lossy on the corpus's own cards or self-refuting. (2) Granularity: the corpus's best-attested optimizer enabler is non-interference around writes (F001, F002), and the precise facts are flow-sensitive spans finer than binding granularity (exclusivity that begins and ends mid-scope). Binding-level mode annotations are necessarily a coarsening; either the compiler re-derives the finer facts internally (inference again) or the optimizer loses F002-class register promotion and read/write elimination precision. (3) Channel architecture: J001 shows the information demand is constant across designs — only the channel differs; dynamic runtimes pay guards and deoptimization (J002) to recover what static derivation yields. F009 shows the attested architecture for carrying high-level facts is compiler-managed IR (Swift SIL), not surface restatement. The sound assumable channel is proof; annotation is a claims channel layered on top of the derivation the checker needs anyway. Derived facts should be emitted to a machine-readable typed-fact sidecar (SIL-style) that the writer and verifier can read back at zero source-token cost. The concession is load-bearing: implicit value-changing conversions silently rebind numeric/mode contracts — under the round1-numeric-semantics leading candidate (named, truthfully-lowered modes; compiler never emits a contract stronger than the source promise), an implicit widening or int-to-float is a silent contract switch, and N002 says overflow facts are contracts, not annotations. Cutting conversions (and conversion-driven overload resolution) is also what makes the kept inference deterministic and near-syntax-directed, bounding its compile-time cost — conversions-times-overloads interaction is the classic type-checker blowup mechanism (not carded; flagged untested). Kept inference is policy-free: it propagates facts from annotated anchors and never chooses among semantically distinct alternatives — no literal defaulting into a numeric mode; ambiguity is a hard error demanding annotation, consistent with numeric-semantics no_decision on defaults. The partial-keep boundary (fully annotated signatures) preserves D004-class separate-compilation/codegen-unit boundaries and firewalls edit amplification at module granularity.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: noalias/alias.scope metadata is UB on violation — annotations in this class cannot be trusted from a fallible writer, so they must be compiler-proven; the proving mechanism is inference/analysis, which therefore stays in the core under either branch of the alternative.

nsw/nuw make overflow poison: mode markings are optimization CONTRACTS, not comments. Grounds both the rejection of trusted mode annotations at every call and the concession that implicit numeric conversions (which silently switch which contract applies) must be cut.
range metadata poisons out-of-range values — another UB-bearing claims channel proving that writer-asserted facts must be proof-gated, i.e., independently derived by the compiler.
'Metadata is sound only if frontend semantics/proofs are sound and preserved' — the annotate-and-trust branch of the alternative violates the corpus's own soundness discipline.

Post-critique convergence of all three positions: compiler-proven facts assumable everywhere, unproven assertion never ambient. An AI annotation is an unproven assertion; the assumable channel is compiler derivation — inference is that mechanism.

Static types and dynamic profiling+guards satisfy the SAME information demand through different channels; the optimizer needs the facts, not the surface restatement. Static derivation supplies them without J002-style guard/deopt costs and without total annotation redundancy.

Documents the guard/deoptimization price of the runtime channel — establishing that statically derived facts are the cheap sound channel, which inference provides from signature anchors without per-use annotation.

Monomorphization constructs concrete per-instantiation types; these compiler-built intermediate types are what 'annotate every binding' would force the writer to spell out — superlinear source growth in combinator chains and a generation fixpoint (types not writable without running the compiler). Its negative scope (code size/compile time unquantified) is honestly carried.
dyn/vtable dispatch adds lookup cost and PREVENTS INLINING — the carded price of nominalizing/boxing closures, which is the escape hatch mandatory nameability forces for unnameable closure types.

Function-typed fields hide representation/call targets and cause indirect storage even in closed programs — the carded optimizer defeat if closures are erased to nameable function types; positive scope also attests inference+specialization recovering performance when types are stable and concrete.

Language-level exclusivity/non-interference is the corpus's central optimizer enabler; the precise facts are proof products of compiler analysis (safe-Rust-style checking), not per-binding surface claims.

The optimization value is proving non-interference around writes — register promotion, read/write elimination, reordering. These consume flow-sensitive spans finer than binding-granularity annotation; binding-level modes coarsen them unless the compiler re-derives (i.e., infers) the finer facts.

Swift SIL attests the architecture where high-level semantic facts live in compiler-derived IR visible to passes — supporting derived-facts-in-IR plus a machine-readable sidecar, rather than restating facts in source at every use.

Codegen-unit/LTO boundaries gate optimization — grounds the partial-keep line: mandatory full annotation at signatures and module boundaries preserves separate compilation and firewalls cross-module edit amplification.

Leading candidate is named, truthfully-lowered numeric modes with no contract stronger than the source promise; implicit value-changing conversions silently rebind modes, so conceding them is required by the corpus's leading numeric architecture.

SPARK's prover-enforced restrictions attest the verification-oriented architecture: invariants discharged by compiler/prover derivation rather than trusted assertions — the same architecture that makes checked (not trusted) annotations, and hence a core inference engine, mandatory.

machineWriterCosts: Token blowup with superlinear growth under the alternative: monomorphized intermediate types nest multiplicatively (D001-class instantiation), so annotating every intermediate binding restates the whole nested type prefix — O(n^2) or worse source growth in adapter/combinator chains where the elided program is linear; every future context window over this code re-pays the tax on re-read.

Call-site restatement of effects/modes duplicates the callee signature at every use: source size scales with fan-in x signature size, and transitive effect sets grow with call-graph depth — the largest annotations attach to the most-called functions.

Edit amplification and drift concentration: one signature change invalidates annotations at every transitive use site; the writer must regenerate many near-identical annotation blocks, and near-duplicate regeneration is exactly the regime where AI consistency drift is worst. Elision reduces the drift surface for each fact from O(uses) to O(1) authoritative statement plus derivation.

Generation fixpoint: compiler-constructed identities (closure types, monomorphized instantiations) cannot be written without first running the compiler, so one-shot generation becomes an iterative fixpoint with the compiler in the loop — multiplying compile cycles per edit (compile-time in the AI iteration loop).

Diagnostic cascades and unresolvable mismatch polarity: one wrong fact yields correlated mismatch diagnostics at every restatement site, flooding the machine-readable diagnostic channel and the writer's context; a mismatch check cannot say whether the annotation or the body is wrong, and a fallible writer resolving the conflict may 'fix' the wrong side. Single-statement-plus-derivation lets the compiler report one root fact with a provenance chain.

Cost the concession avoids: implicit conversions make near-duplicate snippets silently divergent (same text, different inferred conversion path = different mode contract per N002) — cutting them removes a silent-drift channel and simultaneously bounds inference compile-time by killing the conversions-x-overloads search explosion (mechanism not carded; flagged untested).
consistencyRisks: Body-level elision lets near-duplicate code copies in different contexts silently infer different types or modes — drift without a diagnostic; mitigated but not eliminated by policy-free inference where any ambiguity is a hard error demanding annotation.

Silent semantic re-derivation under local edits: changing one binding shifts downstream inferred types with no textual diff at use sites, so a diff-reviewing AI can miss semantic change; requires the derived-facts sidecar to be diffed alongside source.

Inference-failure diagnostics are inherently less local than annotation-mismatch diagnostics; the machine-readable diagnostics requirement needs deliberate engineering (typed provenance chains from annotated anchors), otherwise the alternative genuinely wins on diagnostic locality.
The derived-facts sidecar can drift from source unless regeneration is atomic with compilation; a stale sidecar misleads the writer exactly like a wrong annotation.

Coupling to the dispatch-generics candidate: the unnameable-intermediate argument is strongest under the monomorphization-only leading candidate; if a shared-generics/witness-table path (F009-style fourth model) is later adopted, more intermediates become nameable and that leg of my defense weakens.
The signature firewall must be absolute: any inference leaking across function boundaries reintroduces whole-program action-at-a-distance, the compile-time hazard and drift channel I conceded away.

Even local, policy-free inference in very large AI-generated function bodies is an unbounded-input risk; complexity bounds (syntax-directed checking, no conversions/overloads) must be enforced as language rules, not implementation habits.
empiricalUntested: All claims about AI writer behavior are EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED: the corpus contains no AI-codegen evidence. Specifically: hallucination/drift rates when regenerating near-duplicate annotation blocks; whether elision actually reduces net error rates versus redundant-annotation cross-checking catching writer errors (the alternative's best counterargument — redundancy as error detection — is equally untested).
Quantified token/context-window cost of full mandatory annotation versus signature-only annotation on realistic code; the O(n^2) combinator-chain growth is analytic, not measured.
Whether diagnostic cascades measurably degrade AI repair loops, and whether provenance-chain diagnostics from inferred facts can match annotation-mismatch diagnostics for machine consumption.

Compile-time comparison at scale: policy-free local inference versus checking total annotations, including pathological AI-generated function sizes; the claim that conversions-x-overloads interaction drives type-checker blowups (C++/Swift folklore) has no corpus card.
Whether an AI writer benefits from reading back a derived-facts sidecar versus in-source annotations (the F009/SIL analogy is architectural precedent, not evidence about machine writers).
Whether mandatory-annotation regimes create implementation pressure to trust rather than check annotations over time (sociological claim, no evidence).

The corpus-wide caveat applies: no card quantifies performance, code-size, or compile-time magnitude for any contract regime (caveats.md), and the load-bearing D-cards (D001, D002, D009) are provisional direct extractions not yet adversarially verified per the caveats.md standing rule.

result.stance: CUT. Macros, comptime, and in-language metaprogramming must not be in the core language. The AI writer performs all expansion before source exists; the language has zero staging. Any residual expressiveness gap is closed by checked declarative constructs (guaranteed specialization, contract/fact channels), never by an unchecked code-generating stage.
result.coreArgument: The prosecution rests on four pillars. (1) PROCEDURAL: the corpus's own rules dispose of this feature. The standing rule in notes/caveats.md requires every design proposal to cite exact evidence cards, and implementation/decision-gates.md states a feature cannot become a language default merely because it is attractive. The metaprogramming row of matrices/feature-evidence-coverage.csv is the emptiest row in the matrix: evidence_stage=missing_primary_source, zero workflow-confirmed finding IDs, gap note 'No workflow-confirmed finding yet; do not infer design direction.' The defense has literally no admissible cards; the null hypothesis (not in core) stands unopposed on evidence. (2) SOUNDNESS: this language's entire confirmed value proposition is verified facts above, UB-bearing contracts below (F001/F002/F003; the round1-ir-strategy convergent kernel). The corpus repeatedly disqualifies mechanisms that rest on writer truthfulness without checking machinery — F001's negative scope ('restrict-like contracts depend on programmer truthfulness'), and the ir-strategy critic struck direct-emission precisely because unchecked trusted assertions before UB-bearing emission are disqualifying. A macro/comptime layer is exactly that pattern promoted to a code generator: the checker validates each expansion, but the generator itself is verified nowhere, and a single wrong generator emits N uniformly-wrong, mutually-consistent expansions — the worst failure mode for a fallible-at-scale writer, because uniform wrongness destroys the cross-copy discrepancy signal that verification and differential checking rely on. Fixing this requires typed staging — a second type system and evaluator to specify, verify, and maintain — when the corpus's one custom-compiler-infrastructure data point (F009 negative scope: stale SIL docs and OSSA phase confusion at Apple-scale resources) already warns that even well-funded teams struggle to keep one semantic layer sound. In an optimizer-first compiler, a comptime-interpreter bug is not an ergonomics bug; it is a silent miscompile vector injected upstream of every fact the optimizer trusts. (3) REDUNDANCY: everything metaprogramming buys in human languages is already carried by carded, checked mechanisms or by the writer itself. Compile-time specialization is the dispatch-generics leading candidate — guaranteed static monomorphization (D001) as the core's only implicit path — a checked, declarative compile-time code generator. Value ranges and numeric facts are declarative contract channels (N004 range facts, N002 overflow flags). Staged optimization structure (schedules, progressive vector lowering) is compiler-owned IR machinery in every attested system (A002, A004, A006 — and A006's negative scope expressly warns Halide's DSL staging is not automatically transferable to a general language). Boilerplate elimination — the last remaining use — is the one job the writer does natively: the AI is the macro processor, and verbosity is inadmissible as an objection by charter. Keeping an in-language macro system therefore creates a second, unchecked expansion channel competing with the checked one, and the corpus's dynamic-generation cards (D009/D010: world age, method redefinition, over-specialization cache/compile-time pressure) document how generation-at-large complicates optimizer assumptions. (4) BUDGET: every round-1 topic ended research_needed or prototype_needed partly because engineering cost and compile time are unmeasured everywhere (caveats.md: documentation never quantifies compile-time cost). The verified fact pipeline — pass-consumer validation, metadata survival, fit-gap — is still entirely TBD. A macro expander, hygiene system, comptime interpreter with determinism/hermeticity guarantees, and expansion-aware diagnostics would consume the same finite verification budget while enlarging the trusted computing base, and would put a Turing-complete evaluation stage inside every compile in the AI's iteration loop — an admissible machine-writer cost paid on every cycle. The alternative concentrates all trust in one place: the compiler sees only concrete, fully-expanded code, which is also the corpus's best case for the flagship optimizations, since the dispatch-generics record shows per-instantiation concrete code is precisely what discharges F003 noalias obligations with maximal precision while shared/erased bodies may not. Concrete code in, checked facts through, verified contracts out — no stage in the core.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: The row reads missing_primary_source, 0 workflow-confirmed findings, 'do not infer design direction' — under the caveats.md standing rule (cite exact cards) and decision-gates.md (features must pass gates), inclusion in core is procedurally barred today; the null hypothesis stands.

Negative scope: restrict-like contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness or unsafe assumptions.' A macro/comptime generator is an unchecked writer-trusted artifact upstream of fact emission — the exact pattern the corpus flags as needing checking machinery, multiplied by N expansions per generator.

LLVM noalias/alias.scope contracts are UB on violation and 'only sound if frontend obligations are true.' Inserting an unverified code-generating stage between the checked surface and contract emission widens the surface where false facts become miscompiles.

Two ways: (a) the only confirmed staging precedent in the corpus is compiler-owned IR staging (SIL passes before LLVM), not user-authored source metaprogramming; (b) its negative scope (stale SIL docs, OSSA phase confusion at Apple-scale) is the corpus's lone data point on the maintenance cost of an extra semantic layer — adverse for a small team adding a comptime evaluator.

Monomorphization already provides checked, declarative compile-time code generation — the dispatch-generics leading candidate makes guaranteed static specialization the core's only implicit path, covering the dominant performance use case of macros without a second expansion channel. Its negative scope (unquantified compile-time/code-size) also shows even the CHECKED generator's costs are unmeasured; adding an unchecked one is indefensible.

Julia evidence that generation-heavy designs carry optimizer hazards: Function-typed fields hide call targets, over-specialization causes compile-time and cache pressure — the cost class a comptime facility invites, with no card showing offsetting benefit.

World age and method redefinition show that code-defining-code at compile/run boundaries 'complicates optimizer assumptions' — the only corpus evidence adjacent to in-language code generation is a list of caveats, not benefits.

Halide is the strongest steelman for staging, and its own card confines it: schedule separation lives in a constrained DSL's compiler, and the negative scope says benefits are 'not automatically transferable to a general language.' Staging value attested = compiler-owned, not core-language macros.

MLIR Vector's progressive lowering shows staged program transformation is attested as verifier-checked IR pipeline structure — the load metaprogramming advocates claim for source-level staging is already carried inside the compiler where it can be checked.

Range metadata as a declarative fact channel with poison semantics shows how compile-time knowledge should enter this language: as checked contracts consumed by the optimizer, not as writer-computed code injected by an unchecked stage.

InstCombine's Alive2-proof culture shows the receiving compiler verifies its own transforms; a user-level macro layer would be the only transformation stage in the whole pipeline with no analogous verification story.

Type-system-carried facts (Send/Sync, exclusivity) let the optimizer trust memory properties 'unless types say otherwise' — the corpus's confirmed pattern is facts-in-types, checked per concrete program point; fully-expanded concrete code is the easiest case for these checkers.

machineWriterCosts: COST OF KEEPING (admissible, cut avoids them): comptime execution puts a Turing-complete interpreter inside every compile, adding latency to every iteration of the AI's generate-compile-verify loop; no card quantifies it, and caveats.md notes documentation evidence never quantifies compile-time cost — the burden falls on the defense.

COST OF KEEPING: diagnostics on macro-generated code point at expansion output, not the generator; the AI must solve an extra inference problem (map diagnostic back through expansion) per error, inflating context and re-read tokens. Machine-readable diagnostics require a source-map layer that is itself unbuilt, unbudgeted machinery.

COST OF KEEPING: a hallucinated macro/comptime definition produces N consistent wrong expansions — uniform wrongness eliminates the cross-copy discrepancy signal that differential checking and review depend on, so the writer's fallibility is amplified, not contained (EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED as to rates).

COST OF KEEPING: reading code with macros requires the AI to mentally re-run expansion to know what the compiler will actually see, so every re-read of macro-bearing context costs reasoning tokens and risks divergence between the AI's imagined expansion and the real one (EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED).

COST OF CUTTING (admissible, must be answered): token/context cost of generating and re-reading N expanded near-copies instead of one definition plus call sites; expanded source volume also adds parse/typecheck time, though that path is parallelizable and cacheable while comptime evaluation is sequential inside the compiler (EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED which dominates).

COST OF CUTTING: edit amplification — a pattern change requires the writer to regenerate every expansion site; without a single point of update, partial regeneration is possible and must be caught by the verifier or by non-core deterministic build tooling.

COST OF CUTTING: the generator's intent (the 'macro' the AI had in mind) is not preserved in-language; recovering it on later reads costs context tokens and re-derivation may itself drift (EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED).
consistencyRisks: Drift across AI-regenerated near-duplicates is the defense's strongest card: after a spec change, the writer may update some expansion sites and not others. The prosecution's answer is that drift the checker cannot catch is dangerous with or without macros, so the remedy is widening the checkable-contract vocabulary (the violation-semantics direction: compiler-proven contracts) — but this is a program, not a delivered guarantee; invariants outside the fact vocabulary drift silently.

Macros convert drift-across-copies into drift-within-the-generator: one stale macro silently miscompiles all N sites uniformly. Which failure mode is worse for an AI writer is EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED; the prosecution claims uniform wrongness is worse (no discrepancy signal), the defense will claim scattered wrongness is worse (more sites to audit).

The alternative permits deterministic external generators as non-core build tooling; if the ecosystem leans on them, staging exists de facto without language-level checking — a governance/consistency risk that must be managed by requiring the compiler to see only final, fully-checked source.

ABI- and layout-relevant repeated patterns (e.g., per-type serialization) are consistency-critical exactly where the fact vocabulary is weakest — FFI/ABI is itself a missing_primary_source row in the coverage matrix — so cut-side drift there is currently uncheckable.
empiricalUntested: All claims about AI writer behavior are EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED — the corpus has zero AI-codegen evidence: hallucination/drift rates across near-duplicate expansions vs within a single generator, token-cost economics of regenerating expanded code, and whether AI writers consume expansion-site diagnostics better than generator-site diagnostics.

Compile-time comparison between (a) comptime/macro evaluation in-compiler and (b) larger fully-expanded source volume: no cards on either side; caveats.md notes documentation evidence never quantifies compile-time cost.

Whether guaranteed static specialization (D001-style) plus declarative fact channels (N004-class) actually cover all performance-relevant metaprogramming use cases — the specialization-vs-code-growth trade itself has zero quantitative cards (round1-dispatch-generics evidence gaps).

The metaprogramming coverage-matrix row is empty by research selection, not by adverse measurement (caveats.md: corpus emphasis reflects selection); Zig comptime, Rust proc-macros, Julia @generated, MetaML/typed staging, and Terra are all uncarded — the null hypothesis wins procedurally today and could be re-litigated only after primary-source carding.

The claim that concrete per-instantiation code discharges F003 noalias obligations better than shared/generated-generic bodies is flagged in round1-dispatch-generics as a decisive but not-yet-performed construction-level check.

result.stance: CUT. Human-oriented syntax (infix operators, precedence tables, sugar, elision) must not be in the core language. The core surface should be a fully regular canonical form — fixed-arity, explicitly-parenthesized/structured, one legal formatting, bijective with the first verified IR. Any human-friendly rendering belongs in a non-core, lossless display layer that never feeds the compiler.
result.coreArgument: The null hypothesis stands unchallenged by the entire corpus. Across all 36 evidence cards (F001-F009, A001-A010, D001-D010, C001-C006, J001-J005, N001-N006), not one attributes any optimizer benefit to notation: every confirmed source of extreme performance is a SEMANTIC fact channel — non-interference (F001/F002), IR contracts (F003/F004, N001-N005), explicit iteration structure (A002/A003), static specialization (D001) — all of which are notation-independent and, if anything, are carried MORE faithfully by an explicit canonical form. The defense bears the burden of producing evidence that infix/sugar/elision buys performance; the corpus is silent, so the feature enters the core with zero admissible benefit and nonzero admissible cost. Second, sugar is an extra trust boundary in a compiler whose one non-negotiable is verification. The round1-ir-strategy verdict names the leading architecture as a 'verifier-checked, semantics-preserving IR' and warns that every lowering boundary is 'a fact-loss and miscompile surface.' Desugaring and precedence resolution are exactly such a boundary, inserted BEFORE the first verifiable artifact: the elaborator that turns `a + b * c` into a tree is trusted, not checked, and its output is what the verifier sees — so a precedence or elision mismatch between what the AI writer intended and what the grammar decided is a well-typed, silent semantic error that no downstream verifier can catch, because the verifier only ever sees the post-desugar program. In this environment the writer is explicitly fallible at scale and unchecked invariants are forbidden; a precedence table is precisely an unchecked invariant living in the writer's weights. A canonical form converts that whole error class into machine-diagnosable parse errors or eliminates it (structure is explicit, there is nothing to mis-associate). Third, the corpus's own debates already convicted sugar once: round1-numeric-semantics could not bind bare '+' — the leading candidate is a vocabulary of NAMED, truthfully-lowered modes (wrapping/checked/assume-no-overflow per N002's unflagged-add vs nsw/nuw distinction), with the infix abbreviation's meaning 'deliberately left open.' The sugar is the policy hole; the explicit named operation is the defensible core. N002/N003/N004 show the target IR is per-operation, flag-precise, and UB-bearing; F003 says those contracts are sound only if frontend obligations are true. An elided operator underdetermines which contract is emitted; a canonical `(add.wrap i64 a b)` carries the contract syntactically and makes truthful lowering (the discipline F003 and N006's Alive2-proof culture demand) checkable by near-identity translation validation. Fourth, precedent: the corpus's only static-checking success stories bought verifiability by RESTRICTING the language (F006 SPARK; F001's safe-Rust exclusivity; the arrays-loops verdict generalizes this: 'the only precedents that made such facts checked rather than promised did so by restricting the model'). Cutting notational freedom is the same move applied to the grammar. Fifth, the machine-writer costs the environment admits mostly favor the alternative: one canonical formatting makes semantically-identical code byte-identical — enabling content-hash dedup, incremental-compile caching, exact diffing, and mechanical detection of consistency drift between near-duplicate AI-generated regions, none of which work when one semantic object has N spellings. Fixed-form syntax yields stable node paths for machine-readable diagnostics and cheap, cascade-free error recovery in the AI repair loop, and it deletes the parser/precedence/sugar-elaboration engineering budget from a compiler project whose verdicts repeatedly flag engineering cost as a live constraint — every hour spent on grammar is an hour not spent on the verifier that actually guards soundness. The defense's only real card — token cost of verbose canonical text — is EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED in both directions, bounded, and mitigable by a bijective non-core viewer; the tail risk of silent precedence/elision miscompiles of intent is unbounded and lands exactly where this language can least afford it.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: The confirmed IR-strategy lesson is that performance-critical semantics should be VISIBLE to optimizer passes rather than buried as implicit effects (Swift SIL keeps high-level facts explicit). Sugar and elision are the surface-level analogue of 'implicit effects': they hide which operation/contract the writer meant. The canonical alternative extends F009's explicitness discipline up to the source text itself, making surface-to-first-IR translation near-identity.

LLVM no-alias contracts are 'only sound if frontend obligations are true and metadata survives lowering.' Every desugaring/precedence-resolution step is an additional frontend translation whose obligation-preservation must be trusted or verified; a canonical syntax bijective with the first IR minimizes the number and size of these unverified translations, shrinking the trusted computing base of a verification-first compiler.

LLVM distinguishes unflagged (wrapping) add from nsw/nuw (poison-on-overflow) add per operation. A bare infix '+' therefore underdetermines which optimization contract is emitted — the semantic payload lives in a flag the sugar elides. Canonical named operations (add.wrap / add.checked / add.nsw-under-proof) carry the contract in the syntax, making truthful lowering syntactically checkable.

InstCombine's own discipline is target-independent CANONICALIZATION with Alive2 proofs for flag-sensitive folds — the production optimizer already works by forcing one canonical form and pricing every deviation with a proof. A canonical source syntax applies the identical, corpus-attested discipline at the surface; human-oriented multiplicity of spellings is the opposite of the pattern the evidence endorses.

SPARK achieved verification-oriented, optimizer-friendly semantics by RESTRICTING the language (no non-benign aliasing, side-effect-free expressions). This is the corpus's precedent that cutting expressive convenience to make checking tractable is a winning trade in a verification-first design; restricting the grammar to one canonical form is the same move applied to syntax.

Its negative scope warns that restrict-like contracts 'depend on programmer truthfulness' — the corpus's recurring weak point is unchecked writer-held invariants. A precedence/associativity table is an unchecked invariant held in the AI writer's weights; if the writer's implicit grouping differs from the grammar's, the error is silent and well-typed. Canonical explicit structure eliminates this trust dependency.

MLIR Linalg's optimizer value comes from EXPLICIT structure: operands defining iteration spaces, indexing maps, iterator kinds — verbose, regular, machine-oriented representation, not notation. It is corpus evidence that the performance-bearing layer is explicit structured form; a canonical source syntax is simply that form surfaced, while sugar adds distance the compiler must re-derive across.

The definedness lattice shows the target semantics is an explicit, machine-checkable contract system where transforms must provably move toward more-defined values. A source language feeding this pipeline gains nothing from notational implicitness and loses checkability: implicit/elided constructs must be elaborated into explicit contracts by trusted code before any of N001's discipline applies.

Compile-time is a real, corpus-attested budget (codegen units / incremental compilation / LTO tradeoffs). In an AI iteration loop, compile latency is an admissible machine-writer cost; a trivially-parseable fixed-form grammar removes parser complexity and enables aggressive incremental/content-hash caching that ambiguous, multi-spelling syntax defeats.

machineWriterCosts: Silent intent-miscompile risk: precedence and elision errors by a fallible AI writer produce well-typed programs whose parsed structure differs from intended structure; no downstream verifier can catch them because the verifier only sees the post-desugar tree. This is the single worst failure mode for a language whose premise is that the writer cannot be trusted to hold unchecked invariants. (Rate is EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED; the structural fact that the error class exists and is unverifiable downstream is not.)

Consistency drift amplification: sugar gives one semantic object many spellings, so AI-generated near-duplicates can drift syntactically while staying semantically identical (defeating hash-based dedup, caching, and diffing) or — worse — stay visually similar while differing semantically via precedence/elision. One canonical formatting makes semantic identity equal byte identity, turning drift detection into string comparison.

Verifier-budget displacement: a precedence-climbing parser, ambiguity resolution, sugar elaboration, and their tests consume compiler engineering budget that the round1 verdicts show is scarce and contested; every unit spent on grammar is unavailable for the verifier-checked IR and translation-validation machinery the corpus says is load-bearing. Desugaring also adds one more lowering boundary — per round1-ir-strategy, 'each a fact-loss and miscompile surface.'

Diagnostic degradation in the repair loop: irregular grammars produce cascading, position-ambiguous parse errors; fixed-form syntax yields stable node paths and single-cause diagnostics that are directly machine-readable, shortening the AI's generate-diagnose-repair cycle. (Magnitude EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED.)

Compile-time in the iteration loop: fixed-form parsing is linear, trivially parallelizable, and cache-friendly (content-hash incrementality per canonical formatting); the parse-time saving per run is small, but it compounds across the high-frequency AI edit-compile loop and removes a whole class of grammar-level performance bugs. (Delta magnitude EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED.)

Re-reading cost cuts against the feature too: to re-read sugared code correctly the AI must simulate the precedence table in-context; canonical form makes structure locally explicit, so correct re-reading requires no grammar knowledge. The defense's token-count advantage for sugar is offset by this per-token interpretive load. (Both directions EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED.)
consistencyRisks: Risk of the cut — token/context inflation: canonical S-expression/fixed-form text is plausibly 1.5-3x more tokens for the same semantics, consuming context window faster; long verbose spans may themselves induce AI drift (losing track mid-function, truncated re-reads). This is the strongest surviving defense argument and is EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED.

Risk of the cut — training-distribution mismatch: LLMs are trained overwhelmingly on infix languages; generation accuracy in a fully regular form could be WORSE, not better (more hallucinated structure, unbalanced nesting), inverting the reliability argument. EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED and decisive if true — this is the experiment to run before finalizing.

Risk of the cut — sugar reappears outside the core: if tooling adds a human/AI-friendly pretty-print or authoring layer, and that layer is not provably bijective with canonical form, the precedence/elision trust boundary is silently reintroduced outside the verifier's reach. The mitigation (viewer is read-only or round-trip-verified) must be a hard requirement, not a hope.

Risk of the cut — explicitness spreads the drift surface: fully explicit per-operation modes (no elided defaults) mean AI-generated near-duplicates must repeat mode annotations everywhere; a mode mismatch between copies (add.wrap in one, add.checked in its twin) is a new drift class that defaults would have masked. Canonical form makes it detectable, but detection machinery must actually be built.

Risk kept by the feature — the corpus's own record: round1-numeric-semantics already demonstrates sugar-induced inconsistency at design time (bare '+' unbindable, deliberately deferred); shipping infix without settling such bindings would let the same ambiguity leak into every generated file.
empiricalUntested: AI writers commit precedence/associativity/elision errors at a rate material to correctness (asserted error class exists structurally; its frequency in real AI codegen is unmeasured).
Actual token-count and context-cost delta between canonical fixed-form and infix/sugared surface for equivalent programs, under realistic tokenizers.
LLM generation and re-reading accuracy on fully regular S-expression/fixed-form syntax versus infix syntax (training-distribution effect could favor either side).
That one canonical formatting measurably reduces consistency drift and hallucinated divergence across AI-generated near-duplicate code regions.
That fixed-form machine-readable diagnostics measurably shorten AI generate-diagnose-repair cycles versus conventional parser diagnostics.
That parser/desugarer elimination materially reduces compiler engineering cost or compile latency in the AI iteration loop (direction is clear, magnitude unmeasured).
That a bijective display layer can supply human-oriented rendering losslessly enough that no core-language sugar is ever demanded back (round-trip fidelity at scale unmeasured).

General caveat: the corpus contains no AI-codegen evidence at all, and its silence on syntax cuts both ways — it defeats the feature's claim to admissible benefit under the null hypothesis, but it equally provides no positive proof that canonical form helps; all writer-behavior claims above await the experiments.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: The only carded 'benefit' mechanism for generics is monomorphization: the compiler makes the abstraction disappear into concrete type-specific code. In an all-AI-written language the writer can perform that disappearance at generation time, so the trait layer is a frontend convenience whose compiled output the alternative reproduces directly. D001's negative scope also flags unquantified compile-time/code-size cost that traits incur regardless.

Direct evidence that trait-object/dyn dispatch has optimizer-visible cost: vtable lookup, inlining prevented, optimizations blocked. This is the runtime price of keeping interfaces as a dispatch abstraction in the core.

Keeping dyn dispatch coherent requires a whole restriction subsystem (dyn-compatibility: no Self:Sized, no associated consts, no generic dispatchable methods, receiver limits) — pure specification and compiler surface that exists only to contain the feature on trial, and which still leaves the composition hole the round-1 critic exposed.

Devirtualization recovery is conditional, speculative, guarded, revertible, and option-dependent — so any performance story for interface dispatch rests on recovery the corpus says cannot be promised. Cutting dispatch makes the guarantee hold by construction with zero recovery machinery.

Even 'statically known' interface calls can be blocked from IPA by linking/interposition semantics; interface dispatch couples the core to an undesigned module/ABI/visibility model (also D004). Concrete calls minimize that coupling.

Function-typed fields hide call targets and defeat optimizers even inside closed programs — indirect-target dispatch is the documented defeat class, and traits never solved closure dispatch anyway; the defense cannot claim traits close this hole.

Runtimes that lack static call-target knowledge pay for it with guards, deoptimization, and invalidation machinery (with J001) — the corpus-documented cost of not knowing targets statically, which all-concrete code avoids entirely.

What optimizers actually consume is non-interference facts around writes (register promotion, load/store elimination, reordering) — facts about concrete accesses, not abstraction hierarchies. The alternative keeps exactly this carded fact channel.

The IR fact channel is noalias/alias.scope on concrete code, sound only if frontend obligations are true. The round-1 verdict lists 'can a shared/erased generic body discharge F003 obligations, and at what precision loss' as a decisive UNRUN check — the defense's reuse mechanism is unproven against the corpus's flagship optimization class.

SPARK shows the verification-oriented route: side-effect-free expressions and ownership restrictions on concrete subprograms — precedent that effect/purity law annotations on concrete functions are a workflow-confirmed optimizer-and-verifier enabler, with no interface layer required.

Linalg's optimizer-consumed facts are iterator kinds (parallel/reduction) and indexing maps on concrete operations — with A005 (vectorizer legality needs reductions/inductions/alias facts) and A010 (DO CONCURRENT independence restrictions), this cards the alternative's exact vocabulary: algebraic/effect laws on concrete code are the attested fact channels passes consume.

Alive2-style source/target refinement proofs are operationalized practice in LLVM — mechanized equivalence/refinement checking of concrete code exists, which is the mitigation class for consistency drift across writer-emitted near-duplicate copies.

The corpus's only workflow-confirmed dispatch-adjacent card teaches preserving semantic FACTS (ownership, ARC ops) in IR long enough to optimize — it endorses carrying concrete semantic information, not source-level interface abstraction; the lesson transfers to law annotations, not traits.

The one shipped bounded-code-growth alternative (shared reference-type bodies) rests on a JIT with lazy instantiation and runtime metadata whose negative scope disclaims proof of optimization quality and warns reflection metadata constrains closed-world assumptions — the defense's code-size counterargument has no AOT-transferable card.

consistencyRisks: Near-duplicate drift: N writer-emitted concrete copies of one conceptual algorithm can silently diverge (hallucinated off-by-one in copy 7 of 12); the proposed mitigation — per-copy verification against shared law annotations plus N006-style refinement checks — is plausible but has no corpus evidence of working at AI scale.

Missed-copy staleness: an algorithm change requires regenerating every concrete copy; without machine-readable provenance metadata ('generated-from' links) in the toolchain, one stale copy is a live bug that no compiler check flags, because the copies are legally independent functions.

Later error discovery: interface bounds catch 'type lacks operation X' at declaration; without them the error surfaces as an unknown-function/type-mismatch diagnostic at the concrete call site, possibly after more generation cycles — diagnostics must be precise and machine-readable or iteration count rises.

Uncontrolled instantiation volume: a compiler monomorphizer instantiates only used instantiations (D001); a fallible writer may over-emit copies, inflating code size, i-cache pressure, and compile time (D001/D009 negative scopes) beyond what trait-based compilation would have produced.

Heterogeneity edge: closed sum types + exhaustive dispatch cover heterogeneous collections with all targets visible by construction, but open/plugin-style extension across separately compiled or FFI boundaries has no story — and the round-1 verdict warns the fallback (hand-rolled void*/fn-pointer erasure) is handled WORSE by the optimizer and F003 machinery than a sanctioned construct would be; this risk moves to the undesigned periphery, it does not vanish.

Law-annotation trust: with traits gone, algebraic/effect annotations become the sole cross-function contract vocabulary; if they are writer-asserted rather than compiler-checked, a fallible writer at scale will eventually assert a false 'associative' or 'pure' — the violation-semantics topic (round-1: research_needed) must resolve before these annotations can be assumed by the optimizer.
result.coreArgument: The null hypothesis is that interfaces/traits are NOT in the core, and the defense cannot meet its burden: every load-bearing dispatch card (D001, D002, D003, D005, D007) is a provisional direct extraction, not adversarially verified (caveats.md standing rule; round1-dispatch-generics.md, evidence gaps), and the corpus contains ZERO quantitative cards on the one trade — reuse convenience vs specialization cost — that could justify the feature. What the corpus does card, it cards against the defense. (1) Optimizers consume facts about concrete code, never abstraction hierarchies: non-interference around writes (F001, F002), noalias contracts (F003, F004), purity/side-effect freedom (F006), parallel/reduction/independence structure (A002, A005, A010), numeric flags and ranges (N002, N004). Not one card shows interface abstraction per se enabling an optimization; the dispatch cards document its costs (D002: vtable calls block inlining) and the containment rules it forces (D003), and its sole carded benefit mechanism is monomorphization (D001) — machinery for making the abstraction disappear. In a language where ALL code is machine-written, the writer can make the abstraction disappear before the compiler sees it: emit concrete, specialized functions carrying the algebraic/effect law annotations directly. That is precisely the proposed alternative, and it is the carded fact channel. (2) The feature costs the compiler three subsystems the charter says we cannot afford: trait/instance resolution plus coherence checking (frontend compile time added to the AI iteration loop, on top of monomorphization costs D001/D009 already flag as unquantified); a dyn representation and ABI whose performance recovery is conditional-and-revertible (D007 negative scope, D008, F007, misleading-comparisons note 3 — the corpus's best-attested qualitative asymmetry: source-level guarantees beat recovery-by-optimization); and the round-1 verdict's own open wounds — whether shared generic bodies can even discharge F003 alias obligations is the decisive UNRUN check, the D003 dyn composition hole is unmitigated, and the whole model is coupled to an undesigned build/visibility/interposition story (D004, D008). Cutting the feature deletes all three surfaces and makes the round-1 leading candidate — 'guaranteed static specialization as the only implicit path' — trivially true by construction, without its residual dyn escape hatch, which existed to serve human ergonomics of heterogeneous APIs. Heterogeneity that machines need can be closed sum types with exhaustive dispatch: closed-world by construction, every target visible, no devirtualization required — where A2's closed-world position needed uncarded WPD machinery to approximate the same property, and where J001/J002 show dynamic runtimes paying guards-and-deopt to recover it. (3) Verification, which matters MORE here, favors the cut: generic code verified once against trait bounds makes soundness hinge on instance selection, coherence, and law conformance of every impl — new compiler bug classes for a fallible writer to trip. Concrete functions are verified per-instance against local facts, the same obligation monomorphization creates anyway, and near-duplicate copies are checkable by the refinement tooling LLVM practice already operationalizes (N006, Alive2). Even F009, the only workflow-confirmed dispatch-adjacent card, teaches preserving concrete semantic facts (ownership, ARC ops) — not source interfaces. The admissible machine-writer costs of the cut (tokens, drift, regeneration) are real but are toolchain problems — deterministic expanders, provenance metadata, per-copy law checks — not core-language semantics; and every claim about how AI writers actually behave, on both sides, is EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED because the corpus has no AI-codegen evidence at all. An unmeasured convenience cannot buy its way into a core whose charter is a simple, verifiable, extreme-performance compiler. Cut it; let the law annotations carry the load.
empiricalUntested: ALL claims about AI writer behavior on either side — drift rates across near-duplicates, token/context economics of generating and re-reading N concrete copies, ability to maintain copies under change, effectiveness of generation-time expansion tooling — the corpus contains no AI-codegen evidence whatsoever.

Closed-sum-type/exhaustive-switch dispatch vs vtable dispatch: optimizer friendliness, inlining behavior, and cost are uncarded in the corpus; my claim that enum dispatch is more analyzable than dyn dispatch is inference from D002/D007, not a card.

Net compile-time effect of the cut: deleting trait resolution/coherence saves frontend time but writer-emitted full specialization may add code volume; no quantitative compile-time card exists anywhere in the corpus (caveats.md; round-1 verdict: zero quantitative cards on runtime, code size, i-cache, or compile time).
Whether per-copy verification against shared algebraic law annotations plus Alive2-style (N006) refinement checking actually catches consistency drift at scale on writer-emitted near-duplicates.
Code-size/i-cache impact of writer-side specialization vs compiler monomorphization vs shared bodies — the decisive A1-vs-A3 measurement the round-1 verdict ordered and no one has run.

Whether shared/erased generic bodies can soundly discharge F003 noalias/alias.scope obligations (the verdict's decisive unrun check) — unresolved in both directions; I cite its unresolved status only as burden-of-proof, not as a result.

Whether machine-checkable law annotations (associative, commutative, pure) can themselves be verified rather than writer-asserted — purity-check evidence is explicitly missing from the corpus (round1-index cross-topic gaps), and violation semantics is research_needed.
machineWriterCosts: Token/context inflation: one generic definition becomes N concrete copies that must be generated, stored, and re-read into context on every subsequent edit; admissible and conceded, magnitude unmeasured (EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED).

Consistency drift exposure: the writer becomes responsible for keeping N near-duplicates semantically aligned, a known hallucination surface; the compiler no longer enforces one-definition semantics for them.
Regeneration burden: any change to a conceptually shared algorithm forces regenerating every copy, requiring provenance/'generated-from' tooling outside the core language to even know the copy set.

Loss of checked contract vocabulary: traits let a writer state 'type T provides op X' and fail fast at declaration; without them the failure surfaces later as call-site diagnostics, potentially adding iteration-loop round trips — mitigated only if diagnostics are machine-readable and precise.

Compile-time exposure shifts from trait solving to code volume: if the writer emits more instantiations than a demand-driven monomorphizer would (D001 instantiates only used types), the AI iteration loop pays the difference in codegen time.
Verification workload multiplies nominally: per-copy checking replaces once-per-generic checking; I argue each check is simpler and more mechanical, but the total-cost claim is untested.
result.stance: CUT — interfaces/traits as abstraction should be excluded from the core language; the core keeps only algebraic/effect law annotations (associative, commutative, pure, non-interference) on concrete functions, with reuse handled at generation time by the machine writer and its toolchain.

KEEP — RESTRICTED. Defend interfaces/traits only as: (1) law-bearing contracts with strict coherence (exactly one impl per type per interface) that turn algebraic/effect annotations into one declared contract plus N checkable per-impl proof obligations; (2) bounded generic parameters whose only implicit compilation path is guaranteed static specialization (D001); (3) one explicit, D003-style restricted dyn construct as the sole dynamic-dispatch path; (4) marker/capability interfaces (Send/Sync-class, C004) as type-level facts. NOT defended: inheritance, default method bodies, blanket impls, structural/duck conformance, or any interface feature whose only justification is reuse ergonomics — those are inadmissible under this charter and should be cut.
result.coreArgument: Cutting interfaces does not remove the demand for abstraction and dispatch; it relocates them into AI-hand-rolled duplication and ad-hoc erasure, which is strictly worse on all three admissible axes. OPTIMIZER NECESSITY: heterogeneous dispatch survives the cut as function-pointer/erased-record dispatch — precisely the optimizer defeat D009 documents (Function-typed fields hide call targets even in closed programs) and that round1-dispatch-generics flagged as the predictable failure mode ('hand-rolled void*/fn-pointer erasure that the optimizer and F003 machinery handle worse'). A restricted interface construct is the only carded way to keep runtime dispatch representation coherent (D002, D003) and to feed finality/devirtualization/IPA machinery structured call-target facts (D006, D007) — and D007's negative scope shows backend recovery is conditional and revertible, so structure lost at the source is not reliably reconstructed. Further, type-level capability facts are inexpressible as annotations on concrete functions: Send/Sync-like sharing capabilities (C004) are the leading round1 concurrency candidate, and the round1 index notes non-interference proofs (the F001–F003 fact pipeline, the corpus's best-attested enabler) quantify over race-free executions — cut the trait-shaped capability mechanism and the soundness precondition for the flagship alias facts has no expression vehicle. Cutting interfaces also forecloses the F009-implied fourth model (IR-level generics/witness tables with guaranteed-specialization annotations) that round1 explicitly ordered a dossier on: you cannot preserve interface semantics into the IR if the source never states them. VERIFICATION NECESSITY: this charter says the writer is fallible at scale and optimizer facts are UB-if-false contracts (F003; misleading-comparisons #5: metadata is sound only if frontend obligations are true). The alternative multiplies the trusted surface: laws restated on every concrete function scale as types×operations independent trusted assertions with no shared contract identity — nothing makes merge_i32 and merge_f64 answerable to the same associativity statement, and a generic algorithm verified once against interface laws cannot exist, so verification cost goes multiplicative exactly where falsehood means miscompilation, not just a bug. Interfaces invert this: one named contract, N mechanically-generated discharge obligations. MACHINE-WRITER FAILURE MODES: D001 establishes the compiler already produces concrete-type-specialized code with concrete-code runtime behavior from ONE generic definition, so hand-duplication buys zero performance while paying N-fold generation/re-read tokens, N-copy consistency drift whose blast radius is UB (drifted 'pure'/'associative' annotations feed F003-class assumptions), N duplicate diagnostics per root cause with no shared identity for machine-readable repair, and it destroys the instantiation-identity facts that compiler-side dedup/sharing strategies need (D004 codegen-unit tradeoffs; D005 hybrid sharing; round1's rustc share-generics follow-up) — the compiler cannot cheaply prove two hand-written copies are the same template. Honest concessions: per-copy, statically-specialized hand-duplicates carry the same local optimizer facts as monomorphized copies, so the optimizer-necessity case for the STATIC side rests on instantiation identity, capabilities, and the dyn path — the verification and machine-writer cases carry the rest; all load-bearing D-cards are provisional (caveats standing rule); nothing here is quantified; and every claim about actual AI-writer behavior is flagged EMPIRICAL-UNTESTED below. This keep is deliberately congruent with the corpus's own round1 leading candidate (guaranteed static specialization core + explicit restricted dyn), which already presupposes an interface construct — the alternative on trial is more radical than anything that survived round1.

citedEvidence.howItSupports: Documents that Function-typed fields hide representation and call targets, causing dynamic dispatch and indirect storage even inside closed programs — the exact shape of the hand-rolled fn-pointer/erasure dispatch the alternative forces the AI writer to produce; round1-dispatch-generics notes no design handles this defeat, and the alternative makes ALL residual dispatch this shape.

Trait objects give dynamic dispatch a coherent, optimizer-visible representation (data pointer + vtable) with a stated cost class; its positive scope directly supports making dynamic dispatch explicit via a language construct rather than ad-hoc erasure the optimizer cannot classify.

Dyn-compatibility restrictions show a language can constrain which interfaces are dynamically dispatchable so the runtime representation stays coherent — this restriction mechanism only exists if an interface construct exists; it is the carded escape-hatch design in the round1 leading candidate.

Devirtualization/indirect-inlining/IPA consume structured dispatch and finality facts, and its negative scope shows recovery is conditional, revertible, and option-dependent — so dispatch structure discarded at the source (as under the alternative) is not reliably reconstructed by the backend; source-level structure is the guaranteed side of the asymmetry.

Monomorphization compiles one generic definition into concrete type-specific versions with concrete-code runtime behavior — nullifying the only performance rationale for AI hand-duplication and grounding the claim that generics-over-interfaces cost zero runtime performance versus N hand copies; its negative scope is my conceded unquantified code-size/compile-time cost.

.NET shows a shipped hybrid (specialize value types, share reference-type bodies) that requires generic/interface metadata to exist; hand-duplicated copies destroy the instantiation identity that makes any such bounded-code-growth strategy — or cross-copy deduplication — available to the compiler.
